attributes.stem: A 22-year-old university student with five years of daily marijuana use presents with recurrent episodes of severe nausea and vomiting relieved by hot showers. Neuropsychological testing reveals deficits in attention, verbal memory, and executive function.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the neurobiological mechanism underlying the cognitive effects of chronic marijuana use?
options: Downregulation of cannabinoid receptors in limbic regions
Increased dopamine synthesis in the prefrontal cortex
Upregulation of glutamate receptors in the hippocampus
attributes.explanation:
The correct answer is downregulation of cannabinoid receptors in limbic regions. Chronic exposure to Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC), the primary psychoactive component in marijuana, leads to desensitization and downregulation of CB1 receptors, particularly in regions critical for memory and executive function such as the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. This receptor adaptation disrupts normal endocannabinoid signaling, which modulates the release of multiple neurotransmitters including glutamate and GABA. The resulting alterations in synaptic plasticity contribute to the cognitive deficits observed in chronic users, including impairments in attention, verbal memory, and executive function as seen in this patient.

The incorrect options can be explained as follows:

  • Permanent destruction of serotonergic neurons - While marijuana use affects various neurotransmitter systems, it does not cause permanent destruction of serotonergic neurons. This mechanism is more characteristic of certain stimulants like methamphetamine or MDMA.
  • Increased dopamine synthesis in the prefrontal cortex - Marijuana actually causes transient increases in dopamine release in reward pathways, but chronic use is associated with blunted dopamine responses. It does not increase dopamine synthesis in the prefrontal cortex.
  • Upregulation of glutamate receptors in the hippocampus - Chronic marijuana use typically leads to dysregulation of glutamatergic transmission, but through CB1 receptor-mediated effects rather than direct upregulation of glutamate receptors.
  • Irreversible inhibition of acetylcholinesterase - This is not a mechanism associated with marijuana. Irreversible acetylcholinesterase inhibition is characteristic of organophosphate compounds and certain nerve agents.

The patient's presentation is consistent with cannabis hyperemesis syndrome (cyclical vomiting relieved by hot showers) and cognitive impairments associated with early-onset, chronic marijuana use. The mild obstructive changes on pulmonary function tests reflect the inflammatory effects of marijuana smoke on bronchial epithelium, which contains a higher concentration of carcinogens than tobacco smoke and is typically inhaled more deeply with longer breath-holding.
learningPoints: Marijuana's active component Δ9-tetrahydrocannabinol (THC) acts on cannabinoid receptors CB1 and CB2, with CB1 primarily mediating central nervous system effects.
Chronic marijuana use can lead to downregulation of endocannabinoid receptors, affecting neurotransmitter systems including dopamine, glutamate, and GABA.
Cannabis hyperemesis syndrome is characterized by cyclical vomiting in chronic users and is thought to result from dysregulation of the endocannabinoid system in the gut.
Marijuana smoke contains more carcinogens than tobacco smoke and causes inflammatory changes in bronchial epithelium leading to chronic bronchitis.
Neuroimaging studies show reduced volume in hippocampus, amygdala, and prefrontal cortex regions with chronic marijuana use, correlating with cognitive deficits.
references: Kumar V, Abbas AK, Aster JC. Robbins and Cotran Pathologic Basis of Disease. 10th ed. Philadelphia: Elsevier; 2020. Chapter 9; page 425-426.
